title: We've Been Named the Top 100 Private Clubs in the World by PlatinumClubNet™
excerpt: The American Club Singapore has received Platinum Clubs of the World recognition for the second consecutive year, placing it among the top 1% of private clubs globally.
body:
We're delighted to share that The American Club Singapore has been awarded the Platinum Clubs of the World – City Clubs by PlatinumClubNet™ for the second consecutive year.

The Platinum Club® status represents the pinnacle of recognition in the private club world – placing us among the top 1% of private clubs globally.

This accolade reflects the Club's commitment to service excellence and exceptional Member experiences – made possible by our community and dedicated team.

title: Join our WhatsApp Channels & Stay Connected with Us!
excerpt: The American Club offers multiple WhatsApp channels for Members to receive exclusive promotions, Club updates, and activity-specific information across dining, sports, wellness, and youth programming.
body:
Get the first dibs on Club promotions and exclusive offers!

The American Club Main WhatsApp Channel — your one-stop channel for everything happening at the Club, from dining specials to event announcements.

F&B WhatsApp Channel — be the first to know about new menus, chef's specials, and dining promotions across our restaurants.

Tennis WhatsApp Channel — for clinic updates, tournament announcements, and tennis programming news.

Squash WhatsApp Channel — stay across squash classes, leagues, and social events.

Golf WhatsApp Channel — golf tournaments, course updates, and Club golf activities.

Youth WhatsApp Channel — programming for children and teens, from kids' camps to teen socials.

Gym WhatsApp Channel — fitness classes, personal training, and gym updates.

sên Spa Updates WhatsApp Channel — wellness promotions, new treatments, and spa news.

sên Spa Bookings WhatsApp Channel — quick booking access for spa treatments.

Scan the relevant QR codes on the Club's website to follow each channel.

title: Expat Living Reader's Choice Award 2025: The American Club as one of the Top 5 Best Social Clubs
excerpt: The American Club has been voted one of the Top 5 Best Social Clubs in the Expat Living Reader's Choice Award 2025 — recognising the Club's commitment to service excellence.
body:
We are excited to announce that The American Club has been voted as one of the Top 5 Best Social Clubs in the Expat Living Reader's Choice Award 2025.

Thank you to everyone who voted for us, and to our incredible Members and Team — this achievement is made possible all because of you!

Your continued support of our Club is what motivates us to continue delivering world-class service excellence to every Member and Guest who walks through our doors. We strive to remain a Home Away from Home for generations to come.

Stay updated with what's happening at the Club by joining our WhatsApp Channels or following our Instagram page.

title: Inspiring Encounters: TAC Swimmers Meet World Champions
excerpt: American Club swimmers competed at the World Aquatics Swimming World Cup in November 2024, where they encountered elite athletes — and received a memorable gift from Olympic champion Léon Marchand.
body:
Although the Paris Olympics have concluded, the energy in the swimming world remains electric. In November 2024, the sport's elite gathered at the World Aquatics Swimming World Cup, hosted at Singapore's renowned OCBC Aquatic Centre. The event featured a thrilling short-course format in a modified 25-meter pool, offering a unique twist to the traditional competition setting.

This prestigious event showcased the world's top swimmers, creating an unforgettable experience for local athletes and fans. The American Club's Kai Alldritt, from the Senior Squad, qualified for the 100m and 200m Freestyle events. Despite juggling school commitments and missing preferred events, Kai competed internationally for the first time. Another talented swimmer, Kenzie Kaufenberg, met qualifying times but was ineligible to compete due to age restrictions.

Despite being unable to compete, the World Cup offered an invaluable opportunity for young Kenzie and peers to observe Olympians like Katie Douglass, Regan Smith, Katie Grimes, Duncan Scott, and Léon Marchand up close. Léon Marchand dazzled audiences with commanding performances months after his four-gold-medal triumph at the Paris Olympics, training under legendary coach Bob Bowman.

In a heartwarming moment, Léon's brother Eliott spotted American Club swimmers cheering enthusiastically. Noticing Kenzie's sign, Eliott pointed it out to Léon, who acknowledged the young fans with a wave. As world-class athletes broke records, they took time to acknowledge fans with smiles and waves from the pool deck.

The most memorable highlight came when Léon, awarded a special medal for exceptional performances, surprised everyone by gifting it to Kenzie in the stands. Overwhelmed with emotion, Kenzie held the medal, symbolizing Léon's generosity and encouragement. Scott Fowler described the moment as "one of the greatest displays of sportsmanship I've ever witnessed." Léon's humility and grace exemplify why these athletes inspire us — not solely for achievements but for their ability to uplift others.

The Club's Masters Swim Team proudly competed in the 2025 World Aquatics Masters Championships at the OCBC Aquatics Centre. This group, trained under Head Swim Coach Greg Fasala, showcased talents in individual and relay events. Many competed for the first time on an international stage alongside world-class athletes, underscoring the Club's commitment to nurturing talent and promoting swimming excellence at all levels.

title: Best Coffee Shops to Explore in Singapore
excerpt: Singapore's coffee culture blends traditional kopi stalls with modern specialty cafés. A guide to must-visit coffee shops across iconic neighbourhoods and hidden gems.
body:
Singapore's coffee culture has grown into a vibrant scene, with both traditional kopi stalls and modern specialty cafés dotting the island. Coffee plays an essential role in the everyday life of Singaporeans, serving as both a social activity and a daily ritual.

Discovering Singapore's Coffee Culture

Singapore's café scene is defined by its unique fusion of local traditions and global coffee trends. On one hand, you have the rich heritage of local kopi stalls, where robust coffee served with condensed milk is a staple of everyday life. On the other hand, the influence of third-wave coffee culture has introduced artisanal brewing methods, ethical sourcing, and an emphasis on high-quality, single-origin beans.

Third-wave cafés focus on coffee as an artisanal product. Cafés like Nylon Coffee Roasters and Common Man Coffee Roasters lead this change, offering thoughtfully crafted brews and educating customers on the origin and flavor profiles of their coffee.

Exploring Kopi: Singapore's Traditional Coffee

Found in kopitiams and hawker centers, kopi is brewed from robusta beans roasted with sugar and butter, giving it a rich, deep flavor. It's typically served with condensed or evaporated milk for a sweet and creamy finish.

Common kopi orders include Kopi (with condensed milk), Kopi Siew Dai (less condensed milk), Kopi Gah Dai (extra condensed milk), Kopi O (black coffee with sugar), Kopi O Kosong (black coffee without sugar), Kopi C (with evaporated milk and sugar), and Kopi C Kosong (with evaporated milk, no sugar).

Best Places to Enjoy Kopi

Ya Kun Kaya Toast — robusta-bean kopi paired with their famous kaya toast. Toast Box — smooth, aromatic kopi with condensed milk. Killiney Kopitiam — full-bodied traditional kopi. Chin Mee Chin Confectionery (204 East Coast Rd) — old-school kopi with charcoal-grilled buns.

Iconic Neighbourhood Cafés

Tiong Bahru — Tiong Bahru Bakery for buttery croissants and strong lattes, Plain Vanilla Bakery for cupcakes and smooth brews, The Glasshouse (Chijmes) for minimalist coffee and pastries.

Central Business District — Common Man Coffee Roasters for flat whites and brunch, The Providore Downtown for premium blends in an elegant setting, Punch for cold brews, Sarnies (Telok Ayer) for laid-back flat whites.

Specialty Brews

Nylon Coffee Roasters (Everton Park) for single-origin espresso, Kith Café for laid-back espresso, Alchemist The Heeren for rich espresso, Halcyon & Crane (Paragon) for bold creamy coffee, % Arabica for single-origin precision.

Hidden Gems

Kurasu (Waterloo St) for Japanese-inspired pour-overs, Bearded Bella (Craig Rd) for Australian-style coffee and brunch, Apartment Coffee (Selegie Rd) for intimate single-origin, Sago House Coffee (Duxton Hill) for coffee–cocktail hybrids.

Sustainable Cafés

Chye Seng Huat Hardware (Tyrwhitt Rd), Bettr Barista (New Tech Park), and The Wired Monkey (Amoy St) lead the way in ethical sourcing and eco-friendly practices.

Classic Pairings

Kaya toast with kopi, croissants with cappuccino, avocado toast with flat white, pancakes or eggs Benedict with latte. Singapore's coffee culture is a vibrant blend of tradition and modern innovation — there's always something new to try.

title: Must Try Local Food in Singapore for Expats
excerpt: Singapore offers a rich culinary tapestry reflecting Chinese, Malay, Indian and Peranakan influences. A guide to must-try local dishes and authentic hawker experiences for expats.
body:
Singapore is a true food lover's paradise, offering a rich tapestry of flavors that reflect the country's multicultural identity. With influences from Chinese, Malay, Indian, and Peranakan cuisine, Singapore's food culture is as diverse as its people.

A Myriad of Flavours

Chinese influence shows up in dishes like Hainanese Chicken Rice and Char Kway Teow. Malay heritage shines in Nasi Lemak and Satay. Indian cuisine contributes Roti Prata, Fish Head Curry, and the iconic pulled Teh Tarik. Peranakan fusion brings Laksa and Ayam Buah Keluak — a marriage of Chinese and Malay traditions.

Must-Try Local Dishes

Hainanese Chicken Rice — poached chicken with garlic-ginger rice. Try Tian Tian Chicken Rice at Maxwell Food Centre, famously praised by Anthony Bourdain.

Roti Prata — flaky pan-fried flatbread served with curry. The egg prata with fish curry is a popular order.

Kaya Toast — toasted bread with coconut–egg jam and butter, served with soft-boiled eggs and kopi. Ya Kun Kaya Toast has been serving this breakfast classic since 1944.

Char Kway Teow — flat rice noodles stir-fried with cockles, Chinese sausage, eggs and sweet soy. Best enjoyed at hawker centres for the signature 'wok hei'.

Nasi Lemak — coconut rice with sambal, fried chicken or fish, anchovies and peanuts.

Hokkien Prawn Mee — yellow and rice noodles in a rich prawn stock with squid and lime.

Laksa — coconut-based noodle soup. Visit 328 Katong Laksa for the iconic Katong style.

Chili Crab — large crabs in a sweet-tangy chili-tomato-egg sauce, perfect for dipping with mantou. Try Jumbo Seafood or Long Beach Seafood.

Snacks & Light Bites

Tutu Kueh & Putu Piring — steamed rice cakes with peanut, coconut or gula melaka. Haig Road Putu Piring is Michelin-recognized.

Goreng Pisang — golden banana fritters from hawker centres like Maxwell.

Nyonya Kueh — colourful Peranakan sweets, including Kueh Lapis and Kueh Salat. Try HarriAnns Nonya Table or Bengawan Solo.

Popiah — fresh spring rolls of turnip, carrot, lettuce, egg, peanuts and prawn. Kway Guan Huat has been hand-rolling for 80+ years.

Curry Puff — flaky pastry with spiced potato, chicken and egg. Old Chang Kee has been a household name since 1956.

Desserts & Drinks

Ice Kacang — shaved ice with syrups, evaporated milk and toppings like red bean, attap chee and palm seeds.

Chendol — shaved ice with coconut milk, pandan jelly noodles and gula melaka.

Teh Tarik — frothy pulled tea, a staple at Indian Muslim kopitiams.

Sugar Cane Juice — pressed fresh from the stalk at hawker centres.

Milo Dinosaur — iced Milo topped with extra Milo powder, a local cult favourite.

Dining Like a Local

Maxwell Food Centre — historic Chinatown hawker, home to Tian Tian Chicken Rice and Maxwell Fuzhou Oyster Cake. Old Airport Road Food Centre — Nam Sing Hokkien Fried Mee and Xin Mei Xiang Lor Mee. Lau Pa Sat — Victorian-era hawker centre that transforms into Satay Street at night.

For refined sit-down meals, try JUMBO (Clarke Quay) for Chili Crab and Black Pepper Crab, Spago Dining Room (Marina Bay Sands) for Wolfgang Puck's takes on local dishes, and Toast Box for breakfast in a café setting.

Hawker Etiquette

Reserve a table by 'choping' with a tissue packet. Order at the stall, pay on the spot (cash is common), and return your tray when done. Sharing tables is normal — queue politely; long lines usually mean great food.

Useful Phrases

Auntie/Uncle (respectful address for stall owners), Da bao (take away), Kopi/Teh + variations (Siew Dai / Kosong / Tarik). The OneMap website is a handy tool for finding the nearest hawker centre.

title: Country Clubs in Singapore: Popular Choices Among American Expats
excerpt: Discover popular country clubs in Singapore that cater to American expats — offering community, recreation, and networking opportunities for families relocating to the city-state.
body:
When relocating to a new country, finding a sense of community becomes essential — especially for American expats moving to Singapore. Country clubs offer more than just recreation; they provide a place to forge social connections, build professional networks, and enjoy family-friendly environments that can make the transition smoother.

Tips for Choosing a Country Club

Location matters — proximity to home, workplace and children's schools makes weekend activities and casual meet-ups more convenient.

Amenities and family programs are important — swimming pools, tennis courts, kids' sports programs, dining for children and weekend events.

For expats building professional networks, social and networking opportunities are key — wine tastings, sports leagues and speaker series provide fertile ground.

Membership costs vary significantly. Consider long-term value of activities, amenities and professional connections when evaluating costs.

Popular Country Clubs

The American Club — a cornerstone for American expatriates, often called a 'home away from home'. Located at 10 Claymore Hill, it offers family programming, networking events, state-of-the-art fitness, outdoor pools, and American-style dining. Full membership is open to American citizens and immediate families; associate membership is open to others.

Raffles Town Club (1 Plymouth Avenue) — upscale environment near Tanglin Road with fine dining, spa, wellness and family-friendly spaces.

Singapore Island Country Club (180 Island Club Road) — prestigious among golf enthusiasts, with tennis, pools, and nature trails.

National Service Resort & Country Club (10 Changi Coast Walk) — casual, affordable option with golf, bowling, swimming and family chalets.

The British Club (73 Bukit Tinggi Road) — central/western Singapore option with tennis, squash, swimming, kids' play areas, cultural events and family dining; open to non-British nationals.

Hollandse Club (22 Camden Park) — laid-back, diverse international membership; sports programs for children plus fitness classes and pools.

Tanglin Club (5 Stevens Road) — one of Singapore's oldest, established 1865; selective membership for professionals and diplomats with fine dining and squash.

Mandala Club (31 Bukit Pasoh Road) — contemporary club for creative professionals and entrepreneurs; membership by invitation.

b_together (50 Turf Club Road) — modern, family-focused, eco-conscious club with creative workshops, co-working space, children's play and eco-friendly dining.

Sports-Focused Clubs

Singapore Polo Club (80 Mount Pleasant Road) — horse-riding lessons, polo matches and social gatherings.

Singapore Cricket Club (Connaught Drive) — one of Singapore's oldest sports clubs, offering junior cricket, rugby and soccer programs at the Padang.

Conclusion

When choosing a country club, consider location, amenities, social opportunities and membership costs. The American Club's vibrant, welcoming community caters specifically to American expats with American and Canadian events, family activities and professional networking opportunities.
